summary:
The Sui Dynasty (581–618 AD) was the brief but crucial reunifying dynasty that ended the nearly four centuries of division following the [[event:2047|Jin Dynasty's]] collapse — the Chinese equivalent of Rome's Diocletian, or perhaps its Augustus. Emperor Wen (Yang Jian) reunified China in 589 AD by conquering the Chen dynasty in the south, ending the [[event:2048|Northern and Southern Dynasties]] period. His reign achieved significant administrative reforms including the expansion of the civil service examination system, standardization of weights and measures, and construction programs that laid the infrastructure foundations the [[event:87|Tang Dynasty]] would inherit.

The Sui's most enduring physical legacy was the [[event:2155|Grand Canal]] — an enormous infrastructure project connecting the Yellow River and Yangtze River basins — and the restoration of the Great Wall. However, Emperor Yang (the second Sui emperor) overextended the dynasty catastrophically: three disastrous military campaigns against Goguryeo Korea (612–614 AD) failed at enormous human cost, requiring the conscription and death of perhaps a million soldiers; forced labor for the Grand Canal and other construction projects alienated the peasant population; and excessive taxation triggered widespread rebellion. The Sui collapsed in civil war in 618 AD — less than 40 years after its founding — but its administrative and infrastructural legacy was so foundational that the Tang was essentially building on Sui work. Historians sometimes pair Sui and Tang as one continuous era, with the Sui as the painful foundation and the Tang as the flowering.
